## II. Two Kinds of Disagreement

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** The conceptual core, taught once. Frame-vs-fact, the diagnostic, the spine, and the three axes. Everything downstream just plays its role — nothing re-explains the machinery. Cruxes are named here but detailed after the gallery (§IV), because a lever only makes sense once you can see what it moves.
:::

Most of why this debate feels intractable is that two different kinds of disagreement wear the same clothes.

Some disagreements are **about the world.** Does power-seeking emerge from capable optimization, or not? Is oversight tractable in the time we have? Will a qualitatively dangerous system actually arrive? These have a fact of the matter; evidence and argument bear on them; in principle they can be settled. Call them **cruxes** — they are the high-leverage *nodes* of the [interactive tool](https://lifeuniversesafety.com/doom-assumptions/index.html), the probabilities where worldviews most diverge.

Other disagreements are **about the question itself.** How much do you weight structural priors against current behavior? Over what window are you forecasting? What do you even count as "doom"? These have no fact of the matter — they are framing choices, upstream of any evidence, because they set the rules for what counts as evidence and what is being asked. Call them **axes** — they are the *labels* of the tool: Perspective, Timeframe, Danger. You don't discover your position on an axis; you choose it.

This is the most useful distinction the map offers, because it tells you **which disagreements are negotiable.** Two people who differ on a crux but share their axes are having a productive fight: same question, same evidentiary rules, a different bet about a fact — argue it out, and evidence can move someone. Two people who differ on an axis are barely in the same conversation: they are running different questions, so their p(doom) numbers aren't even comparable, and no pile of evidence reconciles them until the frame is aligned. Hence a blunt diagnostic for any stuck AI-risk argument: **first check whether you share a frame. If you don't, that's the disagreement — stop trading evidence. Only once the frame is shared is it worth hunting for the crux.**

In that light, the rest of the map is built from three things.

**The spine** is the mechanism — the branching tree from the first two posts: does AI raise the danger at all; through one dominant system or many; does that system model what it is doing; does it expect the catastrophe it causes. The gallery in §III is laid out along it.

**The three axes** are the framing choices you bring to the whole tree:

- **Evidentiary standard — priors ↔ evidence.** Do you reason from structural arguments (instrumental convergence, orthogonality, the difficulty of value loading) and treat current model behavior as near-zero evidence about the real risk, or do you update heavily on what systems actually do? This is the most consequential axis, because it decides whether two people can move each other at all — it is why "but current models behave fine" is decisive for one person and irrelevant to another. Beneath it sits an asymmetry that isn't about evidence at all: the two ends weight *which error is worse* — a missed dangerous system versus an unnecessary restriction — differently, so they push the same data toward different loss functions. (The decomposition-first instinct behind this whole project — refusing to bundle all uncertainty into one number — lives at the empirical end of this axis.)
- **Time horizon — the next few years ↔ a century.** Amodei worries over five to ten years; Ord forecasts over centuries. Any definition of doom pairs with any window, and 20%-over-5-years means something entirely different from 20%-over-a-century. Most stated p(doom) figures omit the window, which is much of why they locate nothing.
- **Definition of doom — extinction ↔ permanent disempowerment ↔ large-scale present harm.** The word does enormous work while meaning different things. Critch's 85% and Yudkowsky's ~99% look adjacent until you notice they aren't naming the same endpoint — and someone whose "doom" is present, distributed harm is standing outside this gallery's frame entirely.

**The cruxes** — the object-level bets where movement actually happens — read more clearly once you've seen the positions they move between, so they wait until §IV.
